name: promql
context: PromQL expression language for querying, aggregating, and transforming Prometheus time series.
info: PromQL is the language that powers the Prometheus UI, HTTP query endpoints, recording rules, and alerting rules. It works across instant vectors, range vectors, scalars, and strings, and combines label matching, time selection, aggregation, and specialized functions like `rate`, `increase`, and histogram helpers. Agents need this node whenever they are turning raw metrics into incident evidence or reusable operational policy.
body:
# PromQL

PromQL evaluates expressions over Prometheus time series. The core mental model is the data type flowing through the expression: **instant vectors** are sets of label/value pairs at one evaluation time, **range vectors** are windows of samples per series, **scalars** are plain numbers, and **strings** are rare helper values.

```promql
up{job="node"}
rate(http_requests_total{job="api"}[5m])
sum by (job) (rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])))
```

Metric selectors can match exact labels, regex labels, and negative matchers. Range selectors add `[...]` windows to metrics, while time modifiers shift or pin evaluation time:

```promql
rate(http_requests_total[5m] offset 1w)
http_requests_total @ 1609746000
rate(http_requests_total[5m] @ end())
```

Use aggregations like `sum by (...)`, `max without (...)`, and `count_values(...)` when you need a service-level answer instead of one line per pod, container, or instance. If an expression is both expensive and reused, move it into [[prometheus/rules]] as a recording rule.

sections.title: Data types
sections.body: PromQL works over instant vectors, range vectors, scalars, and strings. Most operational queries start from an instant vector selector like `up` or a range vector selector like `rate(http_requests_total[5m])`. Choosing the wrong type is the root cause of many query errors, especially when a function expects a range vector but receives an instant vector.

sections.title: Selectors and label matching
sections.body: Selectors choose the input series. Exact match uses `label="value"`, regex uses `label=~"value"`, and negative variants use `!=` or `!~`. Combine selectors deliberately to avoid scanning the full TSDB. A common incident pattern is to start broad with `up` or `scrape_samples_scraped` and then narrow on `job`, `instance`, `namespace`, or `pod`.

sections.title: Offset and @ modifiers
sections.body: The `offset` modifier shifts a selector relative to the evaluation time, which is useful for week-over-week comparisons. The `@` modifier pins evaluation at a specific Unix timestamp or the special `start()` and `end()` values for range queries. These modifiers must follow the selector directly, not the entire aggregated expression.

sections.title: Aggregation and functions
sections.body: Use `sum by (...)` or `avg by (...)` to move from per-target telemetry to service-level answers. Counters usually need `rate` or `increase`; gauges often need `avg_over_time`, `max_over_time`, or plain aggregation; histograms need bucket-aware patterns like `histogram_quantile` over bucket rates. If the same expression appears in dashboards, alerts, and scripts, promote it into [[prometheus/rules#recording-vs-alerting]].
